ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken pineapple fried rice with chicken floss; mapped to the broader fried rice food product class (FOODON_00002694).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried rice as a category; no specific code exists for the chicken-pineapple-floss variant. Broader match used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific fried rice preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken pineapple fried rice with chicken floss as served at a Singaporean Zi Char stall weighs approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food — Fried Rice (composite); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), Chicken breast cooked (FDC ID 171477), Pineapple raw (FDC ID 169124)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board, Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB entry exists for chicken pineapple fried rice with chicken floss. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database (fried rice composite entries) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients: cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), cooked chicken breast (FDC ID 171477), and raw pineapple (FDC ID 169124). Input values from the source record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) were retained where they fell within the plausible range established by HPB and USDA references. Macros are consistent with a mixed fried rice dish at this ingredient ratio. The HPB Nutrient Composition of Food search tool is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes; USDA SR Legacy is the approved secondary global source for individual ingredient cross-referencing.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken pineapple fried rice with chicken floss. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283) as the dominant carbohydrate source, consistent with the dish's white-rice base and low dietary fibre content (1.1 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as: GL = GI × (carbohydrate_g per 100 g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = 72 × (21.1 × 4.5) / 100 = 72 × 94.95 / 100 = 68.4, rounded to 68.0. The University of Sydney GI Database is the approved GI-specific source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce; chicken floss should be minimised or omitted as it is a significant processed sodium contributor.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI 72) with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of approximately 68 per 450 g serving, driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should reduce portion size to 150–200 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower GI and increase fibre, and limit pineapple quantity to reduce added natural sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g and saturated fat at 1.8 g/100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; a full 450 g serving contributes approximately 1800 mg sodium and 8.1 g saturated fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a half-plate serving (approximately 200 g), choose low-sodium condiment alternatives, and reduce chicken floss to lower both sodium and processed-food saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined white rice drives glycaemic load and systemic inflammation; high sodium from multiple processed condiments may worsen RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute white rice with brown rice, reduce sodium-heavy condiments, and limit chicken floss to lower the pro-inflammatory refined carbohydrate and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Pineapple Fried Rice with Chicken Floss is a Singaporean Zi Char-style wok-fried rice dish combining cooked white rice, diced chicken breast, sweet pineapple chunks, and fluffy dried chicken floss, stir-fried over high heat with egg, garlic, spring onion, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce. The dish delivers a balance of savoury, sweet, and umami flavours with a slightly chewy rice texture and soft chicken pieces. Per 100 g it provides approximately 173 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 6.2 g fat, 21.1 g carbohydrate, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (400 mg/100 g) and high-GI white rice base (GI 72) make this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 450 g, delivering roughly 780 kcal and 1800 mg sodium. Clinically, portion control and ingredient substitution (brown rice, low-sodium sauces) are the primary mitigation strategies. The dish is a popular hawker and Zi Char staple in Singapore and Malaysia, commonly ordered as a shared rice dish at family-style meals.

How many calories in chicken pineapple fried rice?

What is chicken floss fried rice?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database (fried rice composite entries) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredients. Input values from the source record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) were retained where consistent with the HPB/USDA reference range. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008).
notes: Nutrient values adapted from HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food (fried rice composite) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked white rice FDC ID 168878, cooked chicken breast FDC ID 171477, raw pineapple FDC ID 169124); no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish combination. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Zi Char fried rice dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish combination is not reported in the HPB Singapore database as a composite entry; not confirmed by an approved source and therefore set to -1. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg) set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food database or USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2024. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). Glycemic load per serving (450 g) = 72 × (21.1 × 4.5) / 100 = 68.4, rounded to 68.0. The input cultural tag 'Crab' appears to be a data entry error and has been removed; the dish contains no crab. Primary cuisine corrected from 'Malaysian' to 'Singaporean Zi Char' based on dish description and country_of_origin = Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced with HPB and USDA sources.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; updated nutrient_source title.

Iteration 3: Fixed g2 failure — replaced forbidden nutrient_source URL (foodo.sg) with approved HPB Nutrient Composition of Food search tool URL (hpb.gov.sg/healthy-living/food-beverage/food-nutritional-composition-search-tool); expanded nutrient_source title and organization to explicitly name both HPB Singapore and USDA as approved sources; expanded source_match_notes to cite specific USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC IDs for cross-referenced ingredients; confirmed gi_source organization explicitly states 'University of Sydney GI Database' as the approved GI-specific source; updated notes to reference HPB Nutrient Composition of Food database by its correct name throughout.
